# Weekly Sector Rotation Commentary: 2026-07-12

**⚠️ Not financial advice.** This commentary is auto-generated each week by Anthropic's Claude (an AI model). Brian Beals is not a registered investment advisor, and Anthropic's Claude is not licensed to provide personalized financial advice. The screener is a research and methodology demo, not a recommendation system. Past performance does not predict future results. Do your own research before making any investment decisions.

---

## What the screen said this week

The screen classified the current environment as mid-cycle, based on year-over-year industrial production (INDPRO) running at +1.7%, which sits inside the rule set's steady-expansion band of 0.0% to 4.0%. A yield curve reading of +0.38 (the spread between longer and shorter dated Treasury yields, where positive values are typically read as non-recessionary) reinforced that classification. In short, the macro inputs pointed to steady expansion rather than an early rebound or a late-cycle slowdown.

Technology (XLK) topped the composite at 82.8 and carried the only Buy signal this week. It scored the maximum cycle-fit value of 100.0, a strong seasonality reading of 85.7, and the highest relative strength in the group at 61.2 with a three-month RS of +19.60%. That combination across all three inputs is what pushed it past the Buy threshold of 65. Note that "Buy" here is an output of this rule set crossing a numeric threshold, not a recommendation.

Communications (XLC) was flagged Watch: its cycle fit is maxed at 100.0, but relative strength of 37.1 sits below the confirmation line of 45, so the rules hold it short of a Buy. Its seasonality carries a thin-sample caveat. No sector fell to the Avoid threshold of 40 or below this week, though Healthcare (42.2) and Energy (44.5) sat closest to it.

## Things worth noticing

There is a visible split between cycle fit and price action. Communications has maximum cycle fit but a three-month RS of -13.37%, one of the weaker readings in the table. That gap is exactly what the Watch label is designed to surface: the framework's cycle logic favors the sector while recent relative price behavior has not confirmed.

Seasonality scores are high almost across the board, with several sectors above 80, yet most still land at Hold. That happens because seasonality is only 25% of the composite, so a strong seasonal reading alone rarely moves a sector far. The XLC seasonality figure also carries a thin-sample flag, meaning fewer historical weeks feed that number and it deserves extra caution.

Worth noting too: the backtest edge over SPY since May 2011 is narrow, and Buy-signal excess forward return is just +0.05%, beating SPY 51% of the time. That is a slim margin.

## Methodology reminder

The composite is the weighted sum of Seasonality (25%), Cycle Fit (40%), and Relative Strength (35%). Lookahead bias in the backtest is controlled using FRED ALFRED point-in-time data vintages, so historical macro readings reflect what was actually known then. The backtest performance is a property of this specific rule set over the tested window, not a forecast of future results.
